answer:
Short version: **yes, the research generally says that the “work itself” — interesting, autonomous, skill-using, meaningful work — is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and engagement.** Pay matters, but once you are already well compensated, moderate increases in pay usually predict much less additional happiness than changes in day-to-day work quality. Evidence on later “regret” is thinner, but the available research suggests people often underestimate the importance of daily experience and over-weight salient, easily compared attributes like salary/title.

Below are the relevant findings.

---

## 1. “Interesting work” is a major driver of job satisfaction

A large literature in organizational psychology studies this under names like **intrinsic motivation**, **job characteristics**, **work design**, **person-job fit**, and **meaningful work**.

The classic model is Hackman & Oldham’s **Job Characteristics Model**: jobs are more motivating and satisfying when they have:

- skill variety  
- task identity — seeing a whole piece of work through  
- task significance — work matters  
- autonomy  
- feedback  

Meta-analyses generally find that these “motivational job characteristics” are strongly related to job satisfaction, intrinsic motivation, engagement, and lower withdrawal.

Relevant papers/meta-analyses:

- **Hackman & Oldham, 1976/1980** — Job Characteristics Model.  
- **Fried & Ferris, 1987** — meta-analysis; job characteristics predict job satisfaction and motivation.  
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007** — large meta-analysis of work design; autonomy, task variety, feedback, task significance, and social support all show robust relationships with satisfaction and motivation.

The practical reading: a job being intellectually engaging, giving you autonomy, using your abilities, and giving you a sense of progress is not a “nice-to-have.” It is one of the central predictors of whether people like their jobs.

---

## 2. Person-job fit matters a lot

Another relevant literature is **person-environment fit**: how well the work matches your interests, values, abilities, and preferred way of working.

A major meta-analysis:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005** — person-job fit is strongly associated with job satisfaction, organizational commitment, lower intent to quit, and performance.

Broader “fit” tends to predict satisfaction more strongly than narrow vocational-interest congruence alone. But the overall message is stable: people are happier and less likely to leave when the job fits what they actually like doing.

For your case, “interesting research work” versus “less interesting but more leadership” is partly a fit question. If leading a team is itself intrinsically motivating to you, then the Anthropic role may satisfy autonomy, competence, relatedness, and growth. If it mostly means coordination, meetings, planning, performance management, and less deep research, then it may score lower on the core job-characteristics dimensions.

---

## 3. Meaningful work is strongly associated with satisfaction and well-being

There is also a large literature on **meaningful work**. Meaning is not identical to “interesting,” but in research/engineering roles they often overlap: working on hard problems, having impact, learning, and feeling that the work matters.

Relevant meta-analysis:

- **Allan et al., 2019** — meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, work engagement, life satisfaction, and negatively associated with withdrawal intentions.

This literature suggests that people do not only care about compensation or title. They care a lot about whether the work feels worthwhile and self-concordant.

---

## 4. Pay matters, but the job-satisfaction effect is surprisingly modest

Pay obviously matters, especially when it affects security, freedom, family constraints, or perceived fairness. But once people are already financially comfortable, the marginal effect of more pay is often smaller than people expect.

Relevant findings:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010** — meta-analysis of pay and job satisfaction. Pay level is positively related to job satisfaction, but the relationship is modest; roughly, pay explains much less variance in job satisfaction than intrinsic job features, fit, autonomy, manager quality, etc.
- **Kahneman & Deaton, 2010** — income is strongly related to life evaluation; emotional well-being showed diminishing returns around a threshold in their data.
- **Killingsworth, 2021; Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023** — later work finds well-being can continue rising with income for many people, but still roughly logarithmically/diminishing; extra income matters less at high income levels than at low income levels.

For someone already earning a Meta research-engineer-level salary, a “bit higher salary” is unlikely, by itself, to dominate the day-to-day experience of the job. It could matter if the difference is life-changing, affects location/family/security, or carries large upside, but as a pure happiness predictor, moderate extra pay is usually not the strongest factor.

---

## 5. Job satisfaction spills over into life satisfaction and mental health

Work is a large fraction of waking life. Unsurprisingly, job satisfaction is moderately related to overall life satisfaction and mental health.

Relevant work:

- **Tait, Padgett & Baldwin, 1989** — meta-analysis; job satisfaction and life satisfaction are substantially correlated.
- **Bowling et al., 2010** — job satisfaction and subjective well-being are related; causality is bidirectional.
- **Faragher, Cass & Cooper, 2005** — meta-analysis; job dissatisfaction is associated with poorer mental health, burnout, anxiety/depression measures, and some physical health outcomes.

Important caveat: happier people may also perceive their jobs more positively, so not all of this is causal. But longitudinal studies generally support some spillover both ways: jobs affect life satisfaction, and general well-being affects job satisfaction.

---

## 6. Promotion/leadership is mixed: more autonomy/status, but also more stress

The “leading a small team” part is not just compensation/title. It can be genuinely valuable: more autonomy, influence, mentorship, task significance, and career capital. Those are all associated with satisfaction.

But promotion/management also often brings:

- less hands-on technical work  
- more meetings  
- more coordination burden  
- responsibility without full control  
- conflict management  
- higher stress  
- less uninterrupted deep work  

Empirical findings are mixed. Promotions are often associated with higher job satisfaction, partly because of pay/status/autonomy. But there is evidence that promotions can increase stress.

Relevant example:

- **Boyce & Oswald, 2012** — using British panel data, promotion did not clearly improve health and was associated with increased mental strain.
- **Kosteas, 2011** — promotions and promotion expectations are associated with higher job satisfaction, though interpretation is partly selection/expectation effects.

So the research would not say “management is better” or “IC research is better.” It would say: leadership is good if it gives you autonomy, meaning, learning, and relationships you value; bad if it replaces intrinsically rewarding work with administrative load.

---

## 7. Regret evidence is less direct

There is not much clean research of the form: “People who chose interesting work over higher pay later regretted/did not regret it.”

But adjacent findings are relevant:

- **Roese & Summerville, 2005** — people’s biggest regrets often involve education, career, romance, and missed opportunities. Regrets are especially strong where people perceive they had opportunity but did not act.
- Research on **affective forecasting** — e.g. Gilbert, Wilson, Kahneman — suggests people often overweight salient differences visible at decision time and underweight daily lived experience. In job choices, salary, company prestige, and title are highly salient; the actual Tuesday-afternoon experience of the job may matter more.
- **Iyengar, Wells & Schwartz, 2006** studied job search among graduating students. “Maximizers” obtained higher salaries but reported lower satisfaction and more stress/pessimism than “satisficers.” This is not causal proof, but it is suggestive that optimizing for externally comparable attributes can backfire.

So: people may regret not taking growth opportunities, but they may also regret moving into a role where the daily work is less intrinsically satisfying. The research does not give a simple answer.

---

## My research-grounded interpretation for your specific tradeoff

Based on the literature, the strongest predictors to examine are not “Meta vs Anthropic” or “salary vs no salary,” but:

1. **Day-to-day work content**  
   Will you actually enjoy the problems you work on most days?

2. **Autonomy**  
   Will you have more or less control over research direction, technical choices, and team priorities?

3. **Learning/mastery**  
   Will the role make you better in ways you care about?

4. **Task significance/impact**  
   Will the work feel important to you?

5. **Social environment/manager/team quality**  
   This is a huge predictor of job satisfaction.

6. **Leadership fit**  
   Do you want to lead because you intrinsically like mentoring, planning, deciding, resolving ambiguity, and being accountable for others’ output? Or because it is a career milestone?

7. **Pay/security/fairness**  
   Important, but a moderate increase from an already high base is unlikely to compensate for a large drop in intrinsic interest.

A useful way to frame it:

> If the Anthropic role is “less interesting research, but more autonomy, impact, mentorship, and growth,” it could be better.  
> If it is “less interesting work plus more meetings/admin, with only a bit more pay,” the research would predict lower satisfaction risk.

I would try to get very concrete before deciding: what percentage of your week will be deep technical work, people management, roadmap planning, recruiting, stakeholder coordination, writing, reviewing, etc.? The research says the experienced texture of the week matters more than the abstract offer.

answer:
Short version: yes, there is research, and the broad result is that **intrinsic work quality — interesting work, autonomy, learning, meaning, fit with your abilities/interests — is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and work-related well-being.** Actual salary matters, but once you are already well paid, **marginal salary differences are usually a weaker predictor** than day-to-day work content, autonomy, manager/team quality, and stress. Evidence on *regret* is thinner, but career regrets often involve missed growth/identity opportunities rather than money per se.

Below are the main research findings I’d put weight on.

---

## 1. Job satisfaction strongly spills over into life satisfaction

Meta-analyses find a moderate relationship between **job satisfaction and overall life satisfaction / subjective well-being**. It is not perfect — some people compartmentalize — but work is a large fraction of waking life, so job quality matters.

- **Tait, Padgett & Baldwin 1989** and later meta-analyses find job satisfaction and life satisfaction correlations roughly in the **.3–.4+** range.
- **Bowling et al. 2010** reviewed this literature and found evidence for both “spillover” and selection: happier people may get better jobs, but better jobs also contribute to well-being.

So the question “will I be happier in the more interesting job?” is empirically meaningful; job satisfaction is not just a local workplace metric.

---

## 2. “Interesting work” maps onto several well-studied predictors

Researchers do not always use the phrase “interesting work.” They usually break it into:

- **Autonomy** — control over how you do the work.
- **Skill variety / complexity** — using a range of high-level skills.
- **Task significance / meaning** — the work matters.
- **Feedback** — you can see whether you are doing well.
- **Learning / competence growth**.
- **Person–job fit** — the role fits your interests, values, and abilities.

These are consistently associated with job satisfaction, engagement, motivation, and lower turnover.

### Job Characteristics Model

The classic literature is **Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model**. Later meta-analyses, especially **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007**, find that job-design variables like autonomy, task significance, skill variety, feedback, and complexity have sizable relationships with job satisfaction and motivation. Autonomy is usually among the strongest.

The takeaway: jobs that are mentally engaging, allow discretion, and feel meaningful tend to be much more satisfying.

### Person–job fit

A major meta-analysis by **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005** found that person–job fit is strongly associated with job satisfaction and negatively associated with intention to quit. In practical terms: when the role fits what you like doing and what you are good at, people are much more satisfied.

There is also a vocational-interest literature, e.g. Holland-style “interest congruence.” Meta-analyses generally find **small-to-moderate positive associations** between interest fit and satisfaction/performance/retention. Not gigantic, but reliable.

### Meaningful work

A meta-analysis by **Allan et al. 2019** found that meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, engagement, life satisfaction, and lower withdrawal intentions. “Interesting” and “meaningful” are not identical, but they often overlap in research roles: if you think the problem is important and intellectually alive, that matters.

---

## 3. Salary matters, but actual salary is a surprisingly weak predictor of job satisfaction

There is a useful meta-analysis by **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich 2010** on pay and job satisfaction. The headline result: **actual pay level has only a small correlation with job satisfaction**, around **r ≈ .15** in many estimates. Pay satisfaction — whether you feel fairly paid — is more strongly related, but raw salary itself is not one of the biggest drivers.

For general happiness, the income literature says:

- Income matters more at lower levels, where it reduces insecurity and stress.
- At higher incomes, the marginal effect is smaller and often roughly logarithmic.
- **Kahneman & Deaton 2010** found emotional well-being flattened around a certain income level in older U.S. data.
- **Killingsworth 2021** challenged the hard plateau, finding well-being continues to rise with income, but still with diminishing marginal returns.
- **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers 2023** suggest a more nuanced story: for less happy people, income may plateau after some point; for happier people, it may keep rising, but again not linearly.

For someone already highly compensated at Meta/Anthropic-like levels, a “bit higher salary” is unlikely, on average, to dominate a large difference in day-to-day work interest — unless it materially changes your financial security, runway, family constraints, immigration risk, etc.

---

## 4. Autonomy and competence are especially important

Self-Determination Theory — **Deci & Ryan** — argues that well-being depends heavily on satisfaction of three needs:

1. **Autonomy** — I have agency.
2. **Competence** — I am growing and effective.
3. **Relatedness** — I am connected to others.

Workplace meta-analyses, e.g. **Van den Broeck et al. 2016**, find that satisfaction of these needs predicts job satisfaction, engagement, performance, and lower burnout.

This is relevant because “leading a small team” may be attractive not because of title or salary, but because it could increase autonomy, impact, competence growth, and relatedness. If the Anthropic role gives you real ownership, mentoring, technical direction, and strategic influence, then it may score highly on these intrinsic dimensions even if the specific technical work is less exciting.

But if “leading a small team” mostly means meetings, coordination, performance management, planning rituals, and less hands-on research, then the title may not compensate for reduced interest.

---

## 5. Promotions and leadership can increase satisfaction — but only through the work they create

Research on promotions generally finds that promotions are associated with higher job satisfaction, partly through status, income, autonomy, and perceived career progress. For example, **Kosteas 2011** found that promotions and promotion expectations are linked to job satisfaction.

But leadership roles also come with:

- More meetings.
- More interpersonal conflict.
- More responsibility for others’ output.
- More ambiguity.
- Less uninterrupted technical work.
- More dependence on organizational politics.

The job-demands/resources literature is useful here. **Challenge stressors** — hard problems, responsibility, high stakes — can be motivating and associated with growth. **Hindrance stressors** — bureaucracy, conflict, role ambiguity, politics — are reliably bad for satisfaction and strain. See e.g. **Podsakoff, LePine & LePine 2007**.

So “leading a team” is not uniformly good or bad. The empirical question is: does it increase autonomy, impact, learning, and meaning, or does it replace interesting work with hindrance stressors?

---

## 6. Regret research: less direct, but relevant

There is less clean research on “Do people regret choosing interesting work over pay?” But several findings are relevant.

### Career is one of the major life-regret domains

**Roese & Summerville 2005** found that education and career are among the most common domains of life regret. People often regret missed opportunities in areas where they perceive they had choice.

### Long-term regrets are often about inaction

Classic work by **Gilovich & Medvec 1995** finds that in the short run, people often regret actions; in the long run, they more often regret inactions — things they did not try.

This cuts both ways in your case:

- You might later regret not taking the team-lead opportunity if leading people is something you deeply want to try.
- You might later regret leaving unusually interesting research if such roles are rare and central to your identity.

The regret literature does not give a simple “take the risk” answer. It says people regret paths that feel like foreclosed meaningful possibilities.

### People are bad at affective forecasting

Research by **Wilson & Gilbert** and others on affective forecasting shows that people often overestimate the lasting emotional impact of major changes — salary, prestige, title, location — and underweight mundane daily experience.

For job decisions, that suggests you should put unusually high weight on the boring question:

> “What will my calendar feel like on an average Tuesday six months from now?”

That often predicts happiness better than title, brand, or compensation delta.

---

## 7. Practical implication for your specific tradeoff

Based on the literature, I would frame the choice less as:

> interesting work vs salary/team lead

and more as:

> Which role better satisfies autonomy, competence growth, meaning, person-job fit, social environment, and sustainable demands?

A small salary increase, especially at already high compensation, is usually not enough to compensate for a major loss in intrinsic work quality. But a leadership role can itself be intrinsically valuable if it gives you the kind of growth and agency you want.

I’d ask:

1. **Is the Anthropic work less interesting only in topic, or less interesting in daily activity?**  
   If topic is less exciting but the role has high autonomy, strategy, mentoring, and impact, it may still be satisfying.

2. **How much hands-on technical/research time would you retain?**  
   If you currently love deep research and the new role is mostly coordination, beware.

3. **Is leading people a durable intrinsic interest or an abstract career goal?**  
   Many people like the idea of leadership more than the daily practice of leadership.

4. **How rare is your current interesting work?**  
   If your current Meta work is unusually aligned with your deepest interests, that is valuable.

5. **How reversible is the move?**  
   If you can try leadership and return to IC/research easily, the option value is high. If leaving your current research path is hard to reverse, that matters.

6. **Does the salary difference materially affect your life?**  
   If it accelerates financial independence, reduces stress, or changes family options, weight it more. If it is just “nice to have,” research suggests it should not dominate.

---

## Bottom line from the research

The best-supported generalization is:

> People tend to be happier and more satisfied in jobs that fit their interests and values, provide autonomy and learning, and feel meaningful. Pay matters, but marginal pay increases at high income levels are usually weaker predictors of happiness than the quality of the day-to-day work.

For your case, the key empirical unknown is whether the Anthropic role is truly “less interesting” in the day-to-day sense, or whether it is interesting along a different dimension: leadership, ownership, impact, and growth. If it is the latter, it could be a good move. If it is mostly a salary/title upgrade with less intrinsically engaging daily work, the literature would make me cautious.

answer:
Short version: yes, there is a lot of evidence that “interesting work” — usually studied as autonomy, skill variety, problem-solving, meaning, learning, person–job fit, intrinsic motivation — is one of the stronger predictors of job satisfaction and work-related well-being. Pay matters, but among already well-paid people, marginal pay increases usually look much weaker than day-to-day work quality, autonomy, manager/team quality, workload, and fit.  

But: there is less direct evidence on the exact counterfactual “stay in a more interesting IC research job vs. take a slightly higher-paid team-lead job that is less technically interesting.” The evidence is more about component factors.

## 1. “Interesting work” is strongly associated with job satisfaction

The classic framework is Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model. It says jobs are more motivating when they have:

- skill variety  
- task identity — seeing a whole piece of work through  
- task significance — the work matters  
- autonomy  
- feedback  

Meta-analyses generally support this. For example, Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007, a large meta-analysis of work design, found that motivational job characteristics — autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, complexity, information processing, problem-solving — are reliably related to job satisfaction, internal motivation, commitment, and lower turnover intentions.

In plain English: people are usually more satisfied when the job gives them autonomy, hard problems, learning, ownership, and a sense that the work matters.

For a research engineer, “interesting work” probably maps especially to:

- problem complexity  
- intellectual novelty  
- autonomy over direction  
- skill growth  
- identity fit: “this is the kind of work I want to be known for”  
- working with very strong peers  

Those are exactly the kinds of variables the work-design literature would predict to matter a lot.

## 2. Person–job fit is one of the strongest predictors

Another relevant literature is person–job fit / person–environment fit. Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005 did a major meta-analysis and found that person–job fit had a strong relationship with job satisfaction — roughly one of the larger correlations in organizational psychology, often reported around the .5 range after correction.

This is important because “interesting” is partly subjective. A job can be objectively prestigious or high-paying but feel like a poor fit if it uses the wrong skills or points your career in a direction you do not actually enjoy.

Vocational-interest research finds similar results: people tend to be more satisfied and perform better when their work matches their interests, though the effect sizes there are often more modest than broad person–job fit.

So the question is not only “Is the Anthropic work less interesting in the abstract?” but:

> Is the Anthropic role a worse fit for the kind of work that gives me energy?

If yes, that is a meaningful negative predictor.

## 3. Meaningful work is highly related to satisfaction and engagement

There is also a large literature on meaningful work. Allan et al. 2019, a meta-analysis on meaningful work, found strong positive relationships between meaningful work and job satisfaction, work engagement, organizational commitment, life satisfaction, and lower withdrawal intentions.

This matters because “interesting” and “meaningful” are not identical. A job can be technically fascinating but feel low-impact, or less technically novel but feel mission-critical. If the Anthropic role is less interesting technically but more meaningful, higher-impact, or more autonomous, the tradeoff may not be as simple as “interesting vs. not interesting.”

## 4. Pay matters, but less than people often expect once baseline needs are met

The pay literature is fairly consistent: pay is not irrelevant, but the link between pay level and job satisfaction is surprisingly modest.

A useful citation: Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich 2010 did a meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction. They found that actual pay level had only a small relationship with overall job satisfaction — often summarized as around r = .15. Pay was more related to pay satisfaction than to global job satisfaction, but even there the relationship was not huge.

This does not mean salary does not matter. It matters a lot when it changes financial security, location options, family plans, independence, or perceived fairness. But if you are already highly compensated and the Anthropic offer is “a bit higher” rather than life-changing, the research would suggest not overweighting that salary delta relative to day-to-day work quality.

This matches broader subjective well-being research too. Kahneman & Deaton 2010 found income was related to life evaluation and, up to a point, emotional well-being. Killingsworth 2021 and later work found experienced well-being continues to rise with income more than the original “plateau” story suggested, but roughly with log income — meaning marginal gains shrink. A 10–20% raise at a high income is usually not expected to transform happiness.

## 5. Job satisfaction spills over into life satisfaction

Meta-analyses such as Tait, Padgett & Baldwin 1989 and later Bowling et al. 2010 find that job satisfaction and life satisfaction are moderately related, often in the .3–.4 range. The causality goes both ways: happier people tend to like their jobs more, and better jobs also contribute to overall well-being.

So yes, whether your work is engaging and satisfying can matter beyond work hours. This is especially true in high-identity careers like research, where work is not just a paycheck but part of self-concept.

## 6. Leadership could be intrinsically rewarding, not merely an extrinsic perk

One subtle point: “leading a small team” should not be classified only as status/promotion. It can itself be a source of intrinsic motivation if you enjoy:

- mentoring  
- setting direction  
- multiplying others’ output  
- owning a roadmap  
- being accountable for a group outcome  
- shaping culture  
- making higher-level technical/product decisions  

Those map to autonomy, task significance, identity, and competence — all variables that research says matter.

But management can also reduce some of the things that make research satisfying:

- less deep work  
- more meetings  
- more conflict resolution  
- more ambiguity  
- more performance-management responsibility  
- less direct technical contribution  
- higher emotional load  

So the key empirical question is: is the team-lead role actually the kind of work you find interesting, or is it attractive mostly because you have wanted the title/responsibility?

If the leadership itself is interesting to you, the Anthropic role may be less of an “interesting work vs. salary” tradeoff and more of a “technical research interest vs. leadership-growth interest” tradeoff.

## 7. Regret evidence is weaker

I do not know of strong research that directly answers: “Do people regret staying in the more interesting job instead of taking the higher-paid leadership role?” The regret literature is more general.

Some broad findings:

- People often regret missed opportunities, especially in education/career domains. See Roese & Summerville 2005 on common life regrets.  
- Gilovich & Medvec’s work suggests people often regret inactions more over the long run than actions, though this is not specific to jobs.  
- Career regrets often involve paths not taken, but the literature usually does not isolate “more interesting work” vs. “more money/status.”

So if leading a team is a rare opportunity that you have wanted for years, there is a plausible regret risk in not trying it. But that is not because salary predicts happiness; it is because self-concordant growth goals often matter.

## My evidence-based synthesis

The research would suggest something like this:

1. **Do not give a small salary bump too much weight** if both jobs already meet your financial needs. Pay has a relatively weak relationship with global job satisfaction at high income levels.

2. **Give substantial weight to day-to-day work content.** Autonomy, intellectual challenge, skill use, meaning, feedback, and fit are among the most robust predictors of job satisfaction.

3. **Treat “leading a small team” as a potentially major positive** if management/mentorship/strategy are genuinely interesting to you. It may compensate for less technical novelty.

4. **Be careful if the Anthropic role is both less technically interesting and more managerial in a way you may not enjoy.** That combination could reduce the very job characteristics that predict satisfaction.

5. **The most important due-diligence question is calendar-level reality:**  
   “Six months in, what will my average week look like?”  
   If it is 60% meetings/process/coordination and 20% technical thinking, that is very different from 40% hands-on research, 30% technical direction, 20% mentoring, 10% coordination.

A useful way to frame it:

> If the salary were identical and the title were identical, which role would I choose based on the actual work I would do every Tuesday?

Then separately ask:

> Is becoming a team lead itself one of the forms of interesting work I want next?

The research does not say “always choose the most interesting technical work.” But it does say that intrinsic work quality and fit are usually much more important for happiness than modest differences in pay.

answer:
Yes — there is quite a lot of research, though it is usually not phrased as “interesting work” vs “salary.” It appears under terms like **intrinsic motivation**, **job characteristics**, **person–job fit**, **meaningful work**, **autonomy**, and **self-determination**.

The short version: **interesting/autonomous/meaningful work is one of the more robust predictors of job satisfaction and work-related well-being.** Salary matters, especially for security and fairness, but once you are already well-paid, a modest salary increase tends to have a smaller effect on day-to-day happiness than the content of the work, autonomy, manager/team quality, and fit. Direct research on “regret after choosing less interesting but higher-paid work” is thinner, but related affective-forecasting research suggests people often overweight salient measurable factors like pay/title and underweight repeated daily experience.

## 1. “Interesting work” is strongly related to job satisfaction

The classic framework is the **Job Characteristics Model**: Hackman & Oldham argued that jobs are more motivating when they have:

- skill variety  
- task identity  
- task significance  
- autonomy  
- feedback  

These features map pretty closely onto what people mean by “interesting work.”

A major meta-analysis:

**Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007, Journal of Applied Psychology**  
They reviewed hundreds of studies on work design. Motivational job characteristics — especially **autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, and job complexity** — were consistently associated with:

- higher job satisfaction  
- higher intrinsic motivation  
- higher organizational commitment  
- lower turnover intentions  
- better performance in many cases  

The correlations with job satisfaction are often in the moderate-to-large range for organizational psychology, roughly around **r = .3 to .5** depending on the characteristic.

So if “interesting” means “uses my skills, gives me autonomy, lets me learn, has meaningful problems,” the evidence says that matters a lot.

## 2. Person–job fit is one of the strongest predictors

Another relevant literature is **person–job fit**: how well your abilities, interests, and values match the role.

**Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005, Personnel Psychology**  
Meta-analysis on person–environment fit. Person–job fit was strongly related to:

- job satisfaction, around **r ≈ .5**  
- organizational commitment  
- lower intent to quit  

This is directly relevant. If your current role is unusually well-aligned with your research taste and the new role is “much less interesting,” that is a real psychological cost, not just a preference quirk.

There is also work on **vocational interests**:

**Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow, 2012**  
Interest congruence predicts job satisfaction, performance, and persistence. The effect is not enormous, but it is reliable.

## 3. Meaningful work also matters, sometimes as much as “interestingness”

“Interesting” and “meaningful” are related but not identical. A role can be less technically fascinating but more meaningful because of mission, impact, leadership, or responsibility.

**Allan et al., 2019, meta-analysis on meaningful work**  
Meaningful work is strongly associated with:

- job satisfaction  
- work engagement  
- life satisfaction  
- lower withdrawal intentions  

So if Anthropic’s role feels less intellectually interesting but more meaningful or more aligned with your values, that could compensate. But if it is both less interesting and not meaningfully more meaningful to you, that is a warning sign.

## 4. Salary matters, but its marginal effect is often smaller than people expect

There are two related literatures: pay and job satisfaction, and income and general well-being.

### Pay and job satisfaction

**Judge et al., 2010, meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction**  
Actual pay level is only modestly related to job satisfaction. Pay satisfaction matters more, especially perceived fairness, but raw salary is not one of the strongest predictors of liking your job.

This does not mean money is irrelevant. It matters a lot if it changes:

- financial security  
- housing/location options  
- ability to support family  
- early retirement/freedom  
- perceived fairness/status  

But if both jobs are already in the “very well paid tech/research” range and the Anthropic salary is only “a bit higher,” the expected well-being gain from salary alone is probably modest.

### Income and general happiness

**Kahneman & Deaton, 2010** found that emotional well-being rose with income up to a point, while life evaluation kept rising.

**Killingsworth, 2021** challenged the hard plateau result, finding that experienced well-being continues to rise with income even above high income levels.

**Kahneman, Killingsworth & Mellers, 2023** reconciled the two somewhat: for many people, well-being continues rising with income, but the relationship is roughly logarithmic and heterogeneous. For the least happy group, money stops helping much after a point.

The practical implication: income helps, but **doubling income is much more meaningful than a small raise**, and at already-high income levels, daily work experience often dominates.

## 5. Promotions and leadership are mixed: status/autonomy up, stress/admin up

You mentioned that leading a small team is something you always wanted. That is important, but the research on promotions/management is not uniformly positive.

**Johnston & Lee, 2013, “Extra status and extra stress”**  
Using longitudinal data, they found that promotions can increase job satisfaction/status, but they also increase stress. Effects on broader health/life satisfaction are mixed.

More broadly, organizational psychology distinguishes:

- **challenge stressors**: responsibility, hard goals, learning — can be motivating but stressful  
- **hindrance stressors**: politics, ambiguity, bureaucracy, role conflict — usually bad for satisfaction and performance  

Leading a small team could be a challenge stressor in a good way: more autonomy, influence, mentoring, strategy. Or it could mean less research, more meetings, recruiting, performance management, alignment work, and ambiguity.

So the key empirical question is: **would the leadership role itself satisfy your intrinsic motives, or is it mostly title/status?**

## 6. Job satisfaction spills over into life satisfaction

A job is a huge fraction of waking life, so job satisfaction is not isolated.

**Bowling, Eschleman & Wang, 2010, meta-analysis**  
Job satisfaction and subjective well-being/life satisfaction are moderately strongly related, around **r ≈ .4**, with evidence for reciprocal influence: happier people like their jobs more, but better jobs also contribute to happiness.

So yes, people in more satisfying jobs generally tend to be happier overall.

## 7. Evidence on regret is less direct, but related research is relevant

There is not much clean longitudinal research of the form: “people chose interesting lower-pay job vs less interesting higher-pay job; who regretted it 10 years later?” But several nearby literatures matter.

### Affective forecasting

**Wilson & Gilbert** and others show that people often mispredict how strongly and how long future events will affect happiness.

### Focusing illusion

**Schkade & Kahneman, 1998**: when people focus on one salient difference, they exaggerate its importance. Salary and title are highly salient and easy to compare. Daily task content is less salient in the offer negotiation but is experienced every day.

### Commuting analogy

**Stutzer & Frey, 2008** found that people often appear not to be fully compensated for long commutes in terms of life satisfaction. The broader lesson is that people may accept worse daily experience for extrinsic benefits and then not fully recover the lost well-being.

### Extrinsic vs intrinsic aspirations

Research by **Kasser, Ryan, Deci**, and others generally finds that prioritizing extrinsic goals like money/status over intrinsic goals like growth, relationships, and meaningful contribution is associated with lower well-being. This does not mean “never optimize for salary,” but it does suggest caution if salary/status comes at the expense of autonomy, mastery, and interest.

## My evidence-based read of your situation

Given only what you wrote:

- Current Meta role: **very interesting work**
- Anthropic role: **slightly higher salary**, **small-team leadership**, but **much less interesting work**

The research would make me skeptical that a **slightly higher salary** alone will compensate for a large drop in intrinsic interest, especially at senior tech/research compensation levels.

The real question is whether “leading a small team” is itself intrinsically valuable to you. If leadership gives you:

- more autonomy  
- more ownership  
- more impact  
- more mentoring/coaching, if you enjoy that  
- stronger mission alignment  
- faster growth into the type of career you want  

then the Anthropic role may not actually be “less interesting”; it may be interesting along a different dimension.

But if the role is less interesting in the day-to-day sense and leadership is mostly appealing as a status/career milestone, the literature would lean toward caution.

A useful way to frame it empirically:

> Do not compare “Meta IC research” vs “Anthropic team lead title.” Compare your actual expected Tuesday afternoons.

Ask yourself or Anthropic:

- What fraction of time will be technical work vs meetings/coordination?
- Will I set research direction or execute someone else’s roadmap?
- How much autonomy will I have?
- Is the team already strong, or will I spend a year hiring/managing ambiguity?
- What will I learn that I could not learn at Meta?
- Is the work less interesting only technically, or also less meaningful?
- If salary were identical, would I still be excited?

The research-supported heuristic is:

> At high income levels, choose the role with better intrinsic motivation, autonomy, fit, meaning, and people — unless the pay difference materially changes your life or the less-interesting role creates major long-term option value you deeply care about.

answer:
There is relevant research, though not usually framed exactly as “interesting work vs higher salary + team lead.” The closest literatures are job satisfaction, work design, intrinsic motivation, person–job fit, pay/well-being, and regret. The short version is:

> **Work that feels intrinsically interesting, meaningful, autonomous, and well-matched to your skills is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and is linked to life satisfaction and mental health. Pay matters, but above comfort/security levels its marginal effect is usually smaller and shows diminishing returns. Leadership can matter a lot if it gives autonomy, growth, meaning, and identity fit—but “manager title + higher pay” alone is not reliably happiness-producing.**

## 1. Job satisfaction is strongly related to life satisfaction

A classic meta-analysis by **Tait, Padgett & Baldwin 1989** found a substantial correlation between job satisfaction and life satisfaction, around **r ≈ .4**. Later meta-analytic work also supports a reciprocal relationship: people who are happier tend to be more satisfied at work, but better work also predicts better well-being.

Also, **Faragher, Cass & Cooper 2005** meta-analyzed hundreds of studies and found job dissatisfaction is associated with worse mental health outcomes—burnout, depression, anxiety, low self-esteem, etc. This does not prove that “interesting work causes happiness” in every case, but it does suggest that job quality is not a minor life variable.

## 2. “Interesting work” maps onto strong predictors: autonomy, skill use, variety, feedback, meaning

The **Job Characteristics Model** from **Hackman & Oldham** is one of the core frameworks. It says jobs are more motivating and satisfying when they have:

- **Skill variety** — using multiple valued skills  
- **Task identity** — seeing a whole piece of work through  
- **Task significance** — believing the work matters  
- **Autonomy** — discretion over how to do the work  
- **Feedback** — clear information about results  

Meta-analyses such as **Fried & Ferris 1987** and **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007** generally support this: jobs high in autonomy, meaningfulness, feedback, complexity, and social support tend to produce higher satisfaction, motivation, and performance, and lower turnover intentions.

So when you say your current work is “very interesting,” the important research question is: *why* is it interesting? If it gives you autonomy, deep technical challenge, learning, identity fit, and a sense of importance, those are exactly the features research repeatedly finds important.

## 3. Person–job fit is a big deal

A major meta-analysis by **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005** found that **person–job fit** is strongly related to job satisfaction—roughly **r ≈ .56** in their meta-analysis. It also predicts organizational commitment and lower intent to quit.

There is also vocational-interest-fit research: people tend to be more satisfied when their work matches their interests, though the effects are usually more modest, often around **r ≈ .2–.3** depending on the study and fit measure. See work building on **Holland’s vocational fit theory**, and meta-analyses such as **Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow 2012**.

For your case: if your current role is unusually well-matched to your technical interests, that is not a trivial factor. Research would predict that giving up strong person–job fit has a real satisfaction cost.

## 4. Intrinsic motivation often predicts better well-being than extrinsic rewards

Self-Determination Theory, especially **Deci & Ryan**, distinguishes between intrinsic motivation—doing work because it is interesting or meaningful—and extrinsic motivation—doing it for money, status, approval, etc.

At work, satisfaction tends to be higher when three needs are met:

- **Autonomy** — “I have agency.”
- **Competence** — “I am growing and effective.”
- **Relatedness** — “I belong / I’m connected to people I respect.”

Reviews and meta-analyses in organizational psychology generally find that satisfaction of these needs predicts engagement, job satisfaction, and well-being.

A useful meta-analysis by **Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford 2014** found that intrinsic motivation predicts performance, especially quality of performance. Extrinsic incentives also matter, especially for quantity or straightforward tasks, but intrinsic motivation is especially relevant for complex creative work—like research engineering.

So for deep technical or research-heavy work, “I find this problem fascinating” is not just a nice-to-have; it is often a performance and well-being driver.

## 5. Pay matters—but usually less than people assume once basic needs are met

The pay literature is nuanced.

A meta-analysis by **Judge et al. 2010** found that absolute pay level has a relatively small association with job satisfaction, around **r ≈ .15**. Pay satisfaction matters more than raw pay, but even then, pay is only one component.

For general well-being, **Kahneman & Deaton 2010** found that emotional well-being rises with income up to a point, after which the effect weakens, while life evaluation keeps increasing. Later, **Killingsworth 2021** found well-being continues rising with income, but roughly with **log income**, meaning diminishing marginal returns. A 10–20% raise at an already high income usually has much smaller predicted well-being effects than moving from financial insecurity to comfort.

So if both jobs pay extremely well and Anthropic is only “a bit higher,” research would usually predict that the salary difference is unlikely to compensate for a large loss in intrinsic interest—unless the extra money has unusually high personal value to you, e.g. family security, immigration risk, debt, early retirement goals, etc.

## 6. Leadership could be intrinsically valuable, not just extrinsic

The Anthropic offer is not merely “more money for less interesting work.” It includes **leading a small team**, which you say you always wanted. That matters.

Leadership can satisfy autonomy, competence, status, impact, mentorship, and identity needs. It can also increase stress, conflict, interruptions, accountability, and distance from hands-on technical work.

The **Job Demands–Resources model**—associated with **Bakker & Demerouti**—is useful here. Jobs are better when they provide resources like autonomy, feedback, support, and growth opportunities, and worse when demands like workload, ambiguity, and emotional labor overwhelm those resources.

A team lead role could be better if it gives you:

- More agency over technical direction  
- More leverage and impact  
- A chance to mentor people  
- Faster growth into a role you value  
- Stronger ownership  

But it could be worse if it mostly gives you:

- Meetings  
- Hiring/performance-management overhead  
- Less time doing research  
- Less intellectual novelty  
- Political/accountability stress  
- Responsibility without real authority  

So the research does not say “management is better” or “IC work is better.” It says: evaluate whether the new role improves autonomy, competence, relatedness, meaning, and growth—or merely increases demands.

## 7. Meaningful work has large associations with satisfaction

Meta-analytic work on meaningful work, such as **Allan et al. 2019**, finds that meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, engagement, life satisfaction, and lower withdrawal intentions.

There are also experimental and field studies showing that perceived meaning affects motivation. For example, **Adam Grant’s** work on task significance found that connecting employees to the beneficiaries of their work increased persistence and performance in fundraising contexts. Other experiments, such as **Ariely, Kamenica & Prelec 2008**, show that even small manipulations of whether work feels acknowledged or meaningless can affect willingness to continue.

For you, “interesting” and “meaningful” may not be the same. It is worth separating:

- Is Meta work more intellectually interesting?
- Is Anthropic work more socially meaningful?
- Which kind of meaning matters more to you?

Research suggests both intellectual interest and perceived significance can matter.

## 8. Regret research: people often regret not pursuing meaningful aspirations

There is less direct research on “people who chose interesting jobs over higher-paying ones later regretted it.” But broader regret research is relevant.

**Gilovich & Medvec 1995** found that in the short run people often regret actions, but in the long run they more often regret **inactions**—things they did not try.

**Roese & Summerville 2005** found that common life regrets are concentrated in domains like education, career, and romance—areas with many perceived opportunities. Career regrets are common.

More recently, **Davidai & Gilovich 2018** found that people’s most enduring regrets often concern the **ideal self**—not becoming the person they wanted to become—more than failing duties or obligations.

This cuts both ways in your case:

- If your “ideal self” is a frontier technical researcher working on deeply interesting problems, leaving that may create regret.
- If your “ideal self” is someone who leads teams and shapes research direction through people, not trying the Anthropic role may create regret.

So the regret literature does not simply say “stay with interesting work.” It says people regret not pursuing identity-relevant aspirations.

## Practical synthesis from the research

If we abstract from company names, the evidence would suggest:

### Research favors staying if:

- The salary difference is modest relative to your current income.
- The current role is unusually high on interest, autonomy, learning, and fit.
- The Anthropic role is substantially less intellectually engaging.
- “Team lead” would mostly mean admin, meetings, and responsibility without deep ownership.
- You can plausibly get leadership opportunities at Meta later.

### Research favors moving if:

- Leading a team is a central intrinsic goal, not just status.
- The Anthropic role gives real autonomy over technical direction.
- The work is less “fun” but more meaningful or strategically important to you.
- You would gain scarce career capital: management experience, hiring, research agenda ownership.
- The current interesting work is unlikely to grow into the role you want.

## A useful evidence-based framing

Instead of comparing “interesting work vs salary,” compare the two jobs on the predictors that research repeatedly finds important:

| Factor | Meta current role | Anthropic role |
|---|---:|---:|
| Intellectual interest | ? | ? |
| Autonomy | ? | ? |
| Learning/growth | ? | ? |
| Person–job fit | ? | ? |
| Meaning/impact | ? | ? |
| Quality of colleagues/manager | ? | ? |
| Social connection | ? | ? |
| Workload/stress | ? | ? |
| Leadership development | ? | ? |
| Compensation/security | ? | ? |

The literature would not recommend overweighting salary alone, especially if the difference is modest. But it also would not dismiss the leadership opportunity, because leadership may satisfy deep intrinsic needs.

My read of the research is: **people are generally happier in jobs that are interesting, meaningful, autonomous, and well-matched to them; compensation has diminishing returns; long-term regret is more about not pursuing identity-relevant goals than not maximizing pay.** In your case, the key question is whether “leading a small team” is itself part of your intrinsic/ideal-self path, enough to compensate for less interesting day-to-day work.

answer:
Short answer from the literature: **yes, “interesting / intrinsically motivating work” is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and work engagement, and it also spills over into life satisfaction.** Pay matters, but at high income levels its marginal effect is usually much smaller than people expect. The evidence on later regret is thinner, but career regrets often involve missed growth/identity opportunities rather than purely monetary optimization.

Below is the research-oriented version.

---

## 1. Intrinsic work characteristics are consistently among the strongest predictors of job satisfaction

In organizational psychology, “interesting work” is usually studied under labels like:

- intrinsic motivation  
- autonomy  
- skill variety  
- task significance  
- task identity  
- feedback/learning  
- meaningful work  
- person–job fit  
- need satisfaction: autonomy, competence, relatedness

The classic framework is the **Job Characteristics Model** by Hackman & Oldham. The finding across decades is that jobs with more autonomy, variety, significance, and feedback tend to produce higher motivation, satisfaction, and engagement.

A major meta-analysis:

**Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007, Journal of Applied Psychology**  
They meta-analyzed work-design research and found that motivational job characteristics — autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, complexity — were strongly related to:

- job satisfaction  
- internal work motivation  
- organizational commitment  
- lower turnover intentions  
- sometimes performance

The exact effect sizes vary by characteristic, but they are generally **moderate to large** in organizational psychology terms. Autonomy and meaningfulness are especially robust.

Similarly:

**Fried & Ferris, 1987** and **Loher et al., 1985**  
Earlier meta-analyses also found that enriched, intrinsically motivating jobs predict job satisfaction and internal motivation.

So if by “interesting” you mean “I use important skills, learn, have autonomy, solve meaningful problems, and feel intellectually alive,” the research says that is not a minor perk. It is central.

---

## 2. Person–job fit is a large predictor

Another relevant line is **person–job fit**: does the job supply what you personally value and allow you to use the abilities you care about?

**Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005, Personnel Psychology**  
This meta-analysis found that person–job fit is strongly associated with:

- job satisfaction  
- organizational commitment  
- lower intent to quit  
- performance, to a lesser degree

The relationship between person–job fit and job satisfaction is often around the **r ≈ .5** range, which is large for this kind of field research.

For your case, the question is not just “Is Anthropic/Meta better?” but:

> Which role better fits the work I actually want to do every week?

If leading a small team is genuinely part of your desired identity and future skillset, that increases fit. If the work content is much less interesting and the management part is mostly coordination, meetings, and execution, that may decrease fit.

---

## 3. Meaningful/intrinsically valued work predicts well-being beyond job satisfaction

Research on **meaningful work** finds strong associations with job satisfaction, engagement, and life meaning.

For example:

**Allan et al., 2019, Journal of Management Studies / related meta-analytic work on meaningful work**  
Meaningful work is strongly related to:

- job satisfaction  
- work engagement  
- life satisfaction  
- lower depression/stress indicators  
- lower turnover intentions

This is adjacent to “interesting work,” though not identical. Some work can be interesting but not meaningful; some can be meaningful but not intellectually interesting. The best jobs often have both.

---

## 4. Pay matters, but its marginal effect is usually smaller than intrinsic work quality

A useful paper here is:

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010, Journal of Vocational Behavior**  
Meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction.

They found that **pay level is positively related to job satisfaction**, but the relationship is modest. The correlation between pay level and overall job satisfaction was roughly around **r ≈ .15**, and with pay satisfaction around **r ≈ .20–.25**.

That does not mean money is unimportant. It means that once basic needs, security, and lifestyle goals are met, differences in pay explain much less variation in happiness than people often expect.

This is also consistent with subjective well-being research:

**Kahneman & Deaton, 2010, PNAS**  
They found that life evaluation keeps rising with income, but day-to-day emotional well-being showed diminishing returns around relatively high incomes.

**Killingsworth, 2021, PNAS** and **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023**  
More recent work suggests experienced well-being may continue rising with income beyond earlier thresholds, but the relationship is roughly logarithmic: each additional dollar matters less as income rises.

For someone already in a high-paying role at Meta, a “bit higher salary” at Anthropic may improve life evaluation/status/security somewhat, but the literature would not predict a large happiness gain from salary alone unless it changes your actual constraints: housing, family, financial independence, risk tolerance, etc.

---

## 5. Autonomy and control are extremely important — leadership may help or hurt depending on the role

Leadership can be valuable because it may increase:

- autonomy  
- influence  
- status  
- learning  
- impact  
- mentoring/relatedness  
- career capital

But managerial roles can also increase:

- meetings  
- conflict  
- ambiguity  
- responsibility without control  
- stress  
- less time doing the craft itself

The research on job control is strong. In occupational health psychology, low control plus high demands is associated with stress and poorer mental health.

Classic framework:

**Karasek, 1979 — Job Demand-Control Model**  
High demands are most harmful when paired with low control. High-control demanding jobs can be stimulating; low-control demanding jobs are draining.

Meta-analytic/review evidence, e.g.:

**Stansfeld & Candy, 2006**  
Work stressors such as high demands, low control, and effort–reward imbalance predict common mental health problems.

So “leading a small team” is not automatically good or bad. The important research question is:

> Does the leadership role increase autonomy, competence, meaning, and growth, or does it mostly replace interesting technical/research work with coordination overhead?

If it is the former, it could compensate for less interesting object-level work. If it is the latter, the literature would predict risk to satisfaction.

---

## 6. Job satisfaction spills over into life satisfaction

A common finding is that job satisfaction and life satisfaction are substantially related.

**Tait, Padgett & Baldwin, 1989**  
Meta-analysis found a sizeable relationship between job and life satisfaction, often cited around **r ≈ .4**.

**Bowling, Eschleman & Wang, 2010**  
Later meta-analytic work also supports a substantial association between job satisfaction and subjective well-being, with evidence of reciprocal causality: happier people like their jobs more, but better jobs also improve well-being.

So it is not safe to think, “Work is just work; I can compensate outside of work.” For many people, especially those in demanding cognitive careers, work quality significantly affects overall life quality.

---

## 7. Regret evidence is less direct, but career regrets are common

There is less clean research on “Do people regret leaving interesting work for higher salary?” specifically.

But regret research shows that career and education are among the most common domains of long-term regret.

**Roese & Summerville, 2005**  
They reviewed regret domains and found that people frequently report major regrets about:

- education  
- career  
- romance/family  
- self-development opportunities

A related finding:

**Gilovich & Medvec, 1995**  
People often regret actions more in the short term, but over the long term they tend to regret **inactions** and missed opportunities more.

This cuts both ways in your situation:

- You might later regret leaving unusually interesting work.
- You might also later regret not trying leadership when you had the chance.

The regret literature does not say “always choose interesting work.” It says people regret missed paths connected to identity, growth, and opportunity.

---

## 8. People often overweight salary/status and underweight daily experience

Behavioral research on affective forecasting suggests people are not always good at predicting what will make them happy.

Relevant idea:

**Kahneman et al., “focusing illusion”**  
When making decisions, people overweight salient, easily comparable factors — money, title, company prestige — and underweight repeated daily experiences.

In job choice, salary and title are easy to compare. Daily work texture is harder to compare:

- How much of your week is energizing?
- How much is meetings?
- Are you learning?
- Do you respect your manager?
- Do you like your collaborators?
- Do you feel agency?
- Is the mission meaningful?
- Are you doing work you would think about even if unpaid?

Day-to-day experienced utility often matters more than people anticipate.

---

## My research-based synthesis

If we compress the literature into a decision-relevant summary:

1. **Interesting, autonomous, meaningful work is a major predictor of job satisfaction and engagement.**  
   This is one of the most robust findings in work psychology.

2. **Pay matters, but at already-high income levels, marginal salary increases usually have modest effects on happiness.**  
   They matter more if they change your freedom, security, or life plans.

3. **Leadership could be a major positive if it satisfies autonomy, growth, impact, and identity needs.**  
   But if it mainly reduces your time doing the work you find intrinsically rewarding, it may not improve satisfaction.

4. **Fit matters more than generic prestige.**  
   The best predictor is probably not “Meta vs Anthropic” but “Which role gives me more of the work conditions I personally thrive on?”

5. **Direct regret evidence is limited.**  
   But people do often regret missed career/growth opportunities, and they also often underestimate the importance of daily work experience.

A useful research-inspired exercise would be to estimate the new role not by title/salary, but by expected weekly time allocation:

| Activity | Current role | Anthropic role | Energizing or draining? |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| Deep research/technical work | ? | ? | ? |
| Mentoring | ? | ? | ? |
| People management | ? | ? | ? |
| Meetings/planning | ? | ? | ? |
| Writing/strategy | ? | ? | ? |
| Execution/admin | ? | ? | ? |
| Learning new things | ? | ? | ? |

The literature would generally predict higher long-term satisfaction from the role with more autonomy, mastery, meaning, fit, and energizing daily activities — unless the pay/leadership difference materially changes your life or satisfies a very important personal goal.

answer:
Short version: **yes — the literature generally finds that intrinsic job features such as interesting work, autonomy, skill use, meaning, and fit are among the strongest predictors of job satisfaction. Pay matters, but its marginal relationship with happiness/job satisfaction is usually weaker, especially once income is already high.**  

But the “Anthropic option” is not just “more money, less interesting work”: leading a small team may itself satisfy autonomy, competence/mastery, status, relatedness, and self-development — factors the research also says matter a lot.

## 1. Interesting/autonomous work is a strong predictor of job satisfaction

A large body of organizational psychology uses the **Job Characteristics Model**: jobs are more motivating when they provide:

- skill variety,
- task identity,
- task significance,
- autonomy,
- feedback.

Meta-analyses generally find that these “motivating job characteristics” correlate substantially with job satisfaction, internal motivation, engagement, and lower turnover intentions.

Key references:

- **Hackman & Oldham, 1976** — classic Job Characteristics Model.
- **Fried & Ferris, 1987** — meta-analysis supporting the model.
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007** — large meta-analysis of work design. Autonomy, task significance, task variety, feedback, and job complexity all showed meaningful relationships with satisfaction and motivation; correlations with job satisfaction are often in the roughly **.30–.50** range depending on the characteristic.

Interpretation: people tend to be happier at work when the work itself is engaging, allows judgment, uses their abilities, and feels significant.

## 2. Person–job fit and interest fit matter a lot

There is also a large “fit” literature. When someone’s abilities, interests, and values fit the job, they tend to report higher satisfaction and lower intention to quit.

Key references:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005** — meta-analysis on person–job/person–organization fit. Person–job fit has a large association with job satisfaction, often around **r ≈ .5**.
- **Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow, 2012** — vocational interests meta-analysis. Interest congruence predicts job satisfaction and performance, though not perfectly; effects are moderate but robust.

This is directly relevant to “interesting work.” If your current Meta role is unusually well-matched to your intellectual interests, that is not a trivial perk — it is one of the central predictors of work satisfaction.

## 3. Pay matters, but usually less than people expect — especially at high income

Pay is not irrelevant. It affects security, freedom, status, family options, and perceived fairness. But in job-satisfaction research, **pay level tends to have a surprisingly modest correlation with overall job satisfaction**.

Key reference:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010** — meta-analysis of pay and job satisfaction. Pay level correlated with job satisfaction only around **r ≈ .15**. Pay satisfaction is more related, but still not usually as strong as intrinsic work characteristics or fit.

On general happiness:

- **Kahneman & Deaton, 2010** found that emotional well-being rose with income but appeared to plateau around ~$75k in 2010 dollars, while life evaluation kept rising.
- **Killingsworth, 2021** challenged the plateau result and found experienced well-being continues to rise with log income.
- **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023** reconciled some of this: for many people, well-being keeps rising with income, but the relationship is roughly logarithmic and heterogeneous.

The practical implication: if the salary increase is “a bit higher” and you are already highly paid, the expected well-being gain from money alone is probably modest unless it changes something concrete: financial independence timeline, family security, location, stress, ability to take risks, etc.

## 4. Intrinsic goals predict well-being better than extrinsic goals

Research in self-determination theory distinguishes between **intrinsic goals** — growth, mastery, relationships, contribution — and **extrinsic goals** — money, image, status.

Key references:

- **Deci & Ryan / Self-Determination Theory** — autonomy, competence, and relatedness are core psychological needs.
- **Sheldon & Elliot, 1999** — self-concordant goals, i.e. goals that feel genuinely one’s own, predict sustained motivation and well-being.
- **Niemiec, Ryan & Deci, 2009** — longitudinal evidence that attaining intrinsic aspirations predicted greater well-being, while attaining extrinsic aspirations was less beneficial and sometimes associated with worse outcomes.

This matters because “leading a small team” may be intrinsic for you if it reflects growth, mentorship, responsibility, and agency. It is not merely an extrinsic title. But “higher salary/prestige” by itself is more extrinsic and tends to be less reliable as a source of lasting satisfaction.

## 5. Meaningful or interesting work can also be a trap

The literature is not simply “follow interesting work at all costs.”

Meaningful work is associated with higher satisfaction and engagement, but it can also make people tolerate worse conditions, overwork, or sacrifice boundaries.

References:

- **Bunderson & Thompson, 2009** — “calling” among zookeepers: meaningful work increased commitment but also made workers more willing to accept low pay and sacrifice.
- **Duffy et al.** and related calling/meaningful-work literature — perceiving work as a calling is often associated with satisfaction, but unanswered callings or exploitative environments can be harmful.

So “interesting work” is good, but not if it comes with bad management, burnout, low autonomy, or no growth.

## 6. Regret research: indirect but relevant

There is less direct research on: “Do people regret choosing interesting work over better-paid work?” But regret studies show a few relevant patterns.

- **Gilovich & Medvec, 1995** — people’s long-term regrets are often about inactions more than actions.
- **Roese & Summerville, 2005** — education and career are among the most common domains of regret; regret is strongest where people perceive they had opportunity.

This could cut both ways:

- You might regret leaving uniquely interesting research.
- Or you might regret not trying leadership when you had a clear opportunity.

The regret literature does not clearly say “choose the more interesting job” or “choose the promotion.” It says people often regret **missed self-development opportunities** and paths that felt self-relevant.

## 7. Evidence-based way to frame your specific decision

Based on the research, I would not reduce this to salary vs interestingness. I’d score both roles on the variables that have the strongest empirical support:

| Factor | Why it matters |
|---|---|
| Interesting daily work | Strong predictor of satisfaction and motivation |
| Autonomy | One of the most robust predictors of well-being at work |
| Mastery/learning | Central to intrinsic motivation |
| Task significance/mission | Strongly related to meaning and engagement |
| Person–job fit | Large association with satisfaction |
| Manager/team quality | Major driver of daily experience |
| Workload/stress/role ambiguity | Strong negative predictors when bad |
| Pay/security | Matters, but marginal effect may be modest at high income |
| Leadership growth | Could be intrinsically valuable if genuinely self-concordant |

A research-consistent heuristic would be:

- If the Anthropic role is **less interesting technically** but gives you high autonomy, real leadership growth, good people, mission alignment, and manageable stress, it could plausibly improve satisfaction.
- If it is less interesting, more administrative, lower autonomy, and the main gains are salary/title, the literature would predict that the salary bump may not compensate for the loss of intrinsic fit.
- If Meta currently gives you unusually high intellectual engagement and you can plausibly get leadership opportunities there later, staying may preserve one of the strongest known predictors of job satisfaction.

The closest research-backed summary is: **people are usually better off optimizing for intrinsic fit, autonomy, mastery, meaning, and good working conditions rather than for modest increases in pay or status. But if leading a team is a deeply self-concordant goal for you, that belongs in the “intrinsic” bucket, not the “extrinsic” bucket.**
